case: State v. Kincaid, 83 Ohio App.3d 341 (4th Dist. 1992)
proposition: Outlier reversing OVI conviction where defendant was asleep in the driver's seat with the key in the car ashtray; lead opinion applied pre-2004 McGlone 'key in ignition' formulation literally. Effectively superseded by 2004 statute and by Gill.
brief_block: State v. Kincaid, 83 Ohio App.3d 341 (4th Dist. 1992), is the case at the outer edge: the defendant was asleep in the driver's seat with the key in the car ashtray. The lead opinion, applying the pre-2004 McGlone 'key in the ignition' formulation literally, reversed the conviction; the concurrence — Judge Grey — would have held the ashtray sufficient. Note that Kincaid construed OVI under the old framework that required the key to be 'in the ignition,' a doctrinal element the 2004 statute discarded for physical control. The result in Kincaid would almost certainly come out the other way today under R.C. 4511.194.
opinion_block: Here, the defendant was asleep, the keys were in the ashtray, the motor was not running, the car was legally parked, the defendant's hands were not on the steering wheel and there was no vehicular movement or operator-type conduct. There was no evidence of operation at all. . . . It would appear that where a person is asleep in a parked vehicle, having the key in the ignition is a bright line test for determining if that person is guilty of violating R.C. 4511.19(A)(1). . . . [I]f a person is drunk, asleep in a motor vehicle, with no key in the ignition, and there is no evidence of any conduct which might be included within the broad category of operation, he is not guilty of drunk driving. [Kincaid at 344-46.]
finding_analysis: No direct quotes — no quote check needed. The core factual and doctrinal characterization is supported: Kincaid was asleep slumped over in a parked car, the keys were in the ashtray rather than the ignition, and the Fourth District reversed an R.C. 4511.19(A)(1) conviction on the rationale that 'having the key in the ignition is a bright line test' for the sleeping-defendant scenario. The opinion expressly relies on the pre-2004 McGlone formulation (Kincaid block-quotes McGlone for the proposition that 'an intoxicated person who is in the driver's seat of a motor vehicle parked on private or public property with the key in the ignition is operating the vehicle in violation of R.C. 4511.19(A)(1).'). One small inaccuracy: the memo refers to 'the concurrence — Judge Grey — would have held the ashtray sufficient.' That gets the procedural posture wrong — Grey, J. is the AUTHOR of the lead/majority opinion, and Stephenson, P.J., concurs (in the result), with Abele, J., concurring separately. Grey's opinion is the one that reversed; there is no concurrence reading the ashtray as sufficient. The memo's framing in the proposition itself (that the 'lead opinion applied pre-2004 McGlone key-in-ignition formulation literally') is correct, and the misattribution of Grey to a non-existent concurrence is a stray internal inconsistency in the memo's narrative paragraph rather than a defect in the proposition. The 'effectively superseded by 2004 statute and by Gill' framing is reasonable commentary; no Ohio court has formally overruled Kincaid, but the doctrinal premise (operation requires key-in-ignition for sleeping defendants) is plainly inconsistent with post-2004 R.C. 4511.194 and with Gill's 'potential movement' rationale. Supported on the merits of the proposition, with a minor narrative slip on Judge Grey's procedural role.

proposition: Recent application of R.C. 4511.194 to a driver found unconscious in the driver's seat with keys in the vehicle, with no evidence the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running. Possession-within-the-vehicle treated as established.
brief_block: In contrast, the most recent appellate snapshots — State v. Miller, 2024-Ohio-2009 (2d Dist.), and State v. Adams, 2024-Ohio-174 (2d Dist.) — apply R.C. 4511.194 routinely to drivers found unconscious in the driver's seat with keys in the vehicle (and, for Miller, 'no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running'). Neither decision questions that keys-within-the-cabin satisfies possession. Miller affirmed the OVI conviction on circumstantial evidence of operation, but its discussion of the OVI/physical-control distinction treats possession-within-the-vehicle as established by the keys being inside the cabin.
opinion_block: Miller concedes that he was in physical control of the vehicle but argues that he was not operating the vehicle within the meaning of R.C. 4511.01(HHH). [Miller ¶ 11.] . . . In this case, Miller's blood test following his arrest was positive for methamphetamine at a concentration exceeding the legal limit. The State set forth evidence that Miller was found slumped over the steering wheel in the driver's seat of a vehicle in a ditch on a rural road at around 2:00 a.m. Although there was no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running, Deputy Toomey observed Miller alone in the vehicle, behind the steering wheel . . . . We agree with the trial court that these facts constituted circumstantial evidence that Miller had operated the vehicle to bring it to the side of the rural road where it was found. [Miller ¶ 16.] . . . [T]he trial court . . . noted that the State had not presented direct evidence that Miller had possessed the vehicle's ignition key or other ignition device or that law enforcement officers had observed Miller driving the vehicle. [Miller ¶ 7.]
finding_analysis: The quoted phrase 'no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running' is verbatim against Miller ¶ 16 (VERBATIM confirmed). However, the proposition substantially overstates what Miller holds about R.C. 4511.194 'possession of the ignition key.' Two problems: (1) Miller CONCEDED physical control on appeal. At ¶ 11 the Second District states: 'Miller concedes that he was in physical control of the vehicle but argues that he was not operating the vehicle within the meaning of R.C. 4511.01(HHH).' The opinion's analysis is entirely about whether the State proved the additional element of OVI — 'operation,' i.e., movement of the vehicle — by circumstantial evidence. There is no litigated physical-control holding; the trial court merged the physical control count into the OVI count and the appeal challenged only OVI sufficiency. (2) The 'no evidence that the keys were in the ignition or the engine was running' language is doing OVI-sufficiency work, not physical-control work. The Second District uses the absence of evidence about ignition/engine to frame what the State had to make up with circumstantial evidence of prior movement; it is not saying that keys-not-in-ignition satisfies the 'possession of the ignition key' element of R.C. 4511.194. In fact, the opinion expressly notes (¶ 7) that the trial court itself observed 'that the State had not presented direct evidence that Miller had possessed the vehicle's ignition key or other ignition device.' The memo's characterization — that Miller treats 'possession-within-the-vehicle' as 'established' — has no support in the opinion's reasoning. It rests on the appellant's concession plus a sentence about absence of evidence pulled from the OVI sufficiency analysis. The memo's [HIGH] confidence tag is unwarranted; concession-by-the-appellant is not a litigated holding and cannot stand as appellate authority on the contours of R.C. 4511.194(A)(2). Partially supported: the factual setup (defendant unconscious behind the wheel, no evidence of key-in-ignition) and the verbatim quote are accurate, but the doctrinal characterization — that the Second District treats keys-within-the-cabin as satisfying R.C. 4511.194 possession — extends beyond what the opinion actually decides.

proposition: Recent application of R.C. 4511.194 to an impaired driver found unconscious in the driver's seat; possession-within-the-vehicle treated as established by the keys being inside the cabin.
brief_block: In contrast, the most recent appellate snapshots — State v. Miller, 2024-Ohio-2009 (2d Dist.), and State v. Adams, 2024-Ohio-174 (2d Dist.) — apply R.C. 4511.194 routinely to drivers found unconscious in the driver's seat with keys in the vehicle . . . . Neither decision questions that keys-within-the-cabin satisfies possession.
opinion_block: John Adams appeals from his conviction on one count of possession of drugs following a no contest plea; he argues that the trial court erred in overruling his motion to suppress. For the following reasons, we affirm the judgment of the trial court. [Adams ¶ 1.] . . . The pickup truck was running and in drive, with Adams's foot on the brake enough to keep him from rolling forward. Medical personnel put the vehicle in park and removed the keys for safety. [Adams ¶ 16.] . . . After the investigation, the officers issued Adams a citation for having physical control of the vehicle while under the influence. [Adams ¶ 18.]
finding_analysis: Adams is NOT a R.C. 4511.194 case in any substantive sense, and the memo's characterization of it as a 'recent application' of the physical-control statute is incorrect. The metadata-check flag is confirmed: Adams's sole assignment of error is that 'the trial court erred in overruling his motion to suppress,' and the conviction on appeal is for POSSESSION OF HEROIN (¶ 1), not for R.C. 4511.194 physical control. The opinion's entire substantive analysis (¶¶ 7-23) is Fourth Amendment doctrine — community caretaking / emergency aid, Terry stop, plain view, automobile exception. R.C. 4511.194 appears in only two contexts: (a) the officers' subjective basis for continuing the encounter (suspicion that Adams had 'operated the vehicle while impaired'), and (b) the citation Adams was issued (¶ 18). The court does not interpret 'possession of the ignition key,' does not analyze whether Adams had 'physical control' as a doctrinal matter, and does not address the elements of R.C. 4511.194 at all. Beyond the legal-subject mismatch, the memo's factual framing of Adams is also wrong on the facts. The memo says Adams supports the proposition that 'possession-within-the-vehicle treated as established by the keys being inside the cabin.' But Adams's vehicle was RUNNING AND IN DRIVE with his foot on the brake (¶ 16); the keys were necessarily in the ignition. Medics had to put the truck in park and remove the keys for safety. So Adams is not even a 'keys-inside-the-cabin-but-not-in-ignition' case — it is a keys-in-ignition, vehicle-running case where the only litigated issue was a suppression motion on a separate drug charge. Adams cannot bear the weight the memo places on it. Not supported. This is a 'case on unrelated subject' problem: the Adams court did not address R.C. 4511.194 possession at all, and the factual hook the memo uses (keys-inside-the-cabin) is also factually inapposite. The proposition tagged this with [HIGH] confidence; that tag is unjustified.

proposition: General Ohio constructive-possession standard: constructive possession exists when an individual knowingly exercises dominion and control over an object even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession.
brief_block: Ohio criminal practice generally recognizes both actual and constructive possession — i.e., that "[c]onstructive possession exists when an individual knowingly exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession." State v. Hankerson, 70 Ohio St.2d 87, syllabus (1982). Whether the Hankerson concept of constructive possession imports cleanly into R.C. 4511.194 is unsettled at the appellate level . . . .
opinion_block: Constructive possession exists when an individual exercises dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession. State v. Wolery, supra. . . . [T]he mere fact that property is located within premises under one's control does not, of itself, constitute constructive possession. It must also be shown that the person was conscious of the presence of the object. [Hankerson at 91.]
finding_analysis: The proposition is substantively supported — Hankerson is correctly characterized as the leading Ohio Supreme Court articulation of constructive possession, and the memo appropriately flags (in the surrounding text and again in Part IV) that the import of this doctrine into R.C. 4511.194 is unsettled. But the quoted text is REWORDED, not verbatim. The opinion's actual sentence (at p. 91) reads: 'Constructive possession exists when an individual EXERCISES dominion and control over an object, even though that object may not be within his immediate physical possession.' The memo's quote inserts the word 'knowingly' between 'individual' and 'exercises' — 'Constructive possession exists when an individual KNOWINGLY exercises dominion and control over an object . . . .' This is a substantive insertion, not OCR noise. In Hankerson the conscious-awareness requirement is stated SEPARATELY in the next paragraph ('It must also be shown that the person was conscious of the presence of the object'); it is not part of the constructive-possession definition itself, which Hankerson lifts from State v. Wolery (1976). By splicing 'knowingly' into the definitional sentence, the memo collapses two distinct holdings into one and presents a paraphrase as a direct quote. Two additional notes: (1) The memo cites this to the 'syllabus' of Hankerson; the available opinion file does not include a separate syllabus block, and the quoted definition appears in the body of the Sweeney, J., majority opinion at page 91, not in any syllabus paragraph. The case's actual syllabus paragraphs (per Bluebook practice for older Ohio Supreme Court opinions) are about receiving stolen property and circumstantial-evidence sufficiency, not the abstract constructive-possession definition. (2) Hankerson was a RECEIVING-STOLEN-PROPERTY case (stolen stereo speakers found in the parents' home), not the 'drug-possession' framing that some constructive-possession discussions adopt — the memo does not assert that mischaracterization in this row, but readers should not infer that Hankerson is itself a drug case. Overall: the substantive proposition (general Ohio constructive-possession standard, not yet imported into R.C. 4511.194) is supported, but the quoted text is a paraphrase presented as a verbatim quote, so the row warrants the 'Reworded' yellow badge. The ~0.76 similarity flag from the metadata pass is accurate; this is not OCR noise but a real wording change.
